body:
This PR includes the following changes:

- The addition of "pointwise stencil" operations. These are a generalized form of finite difference operators, where the stencil used to determine the output values can be different at every point. To use a pointwise stencil, one needs a `Field` that stores the stencil; this is currently implemented as a `Field` of `StencilCoefs`, where each `StencilCoefs` object stores the stencil coefficients for a particular output point (in other words, it stores the row of the stencil matrix corresponding to that point). To simplify the creation of stencil fields, I have defined the `Operator2Stencil` operator, which wraps any operator defined in `finitedifference.jl` and outputs the stencil field that corresponds to that operator. Currently supported operations on stencils include `ApplyStencil` (which is effectively a matrix-vector multiplication) and `ComposeStencils` (which is a matrix-matrix multiplication). `ComposeStencils` allows one to specify the Jacobians of arbitrary finite difference broadcasts, since the chain rule can be used to express any Jacobian as a chain of matrix-matrix multiplications, where each matrix corresponds to a particular finite difference operator.

- The modification of `boundary_width` for all finite difference operators. While it previously only accepted the operator and the boundary location, it now also accepts the inputs of the operator. This allows `boundary_width` to be computed for `ApplyStencil` and `ComposeStencils` based on their input stencil fields.

- The simplification of `examples/3dsphere`. The various driver files are replaced with a single `driver.jl`. A flag at the top of this file specifies which initial conditions and explicit tendency function to use. Another file called `utilities.jl` specifies the implicit tendency function and Jacobian function, which are the same for all our tests. Another file called `schur_comlement_W.jl` defines the actual data structure for storing the Jacobian and the linear solver.

- Automatic tests for the implicit solver. The data structure for storing the Jacobian now includes a `test` flag. When this flag is true, a test is run on every Jacobian computation that compares the Jacobian of the first column of the domain to its exact value (determined using automatic differentiation), and another test is run on every linear solve that compares the result of the linear solve to its exact value (determined by using `ldiv!` on regular arrays).

- Some minor bug fixes: 1) A method was added for `RecursiveApply.radd(x) = x`. This was to simplify the use of `radd` in `ApplyStencil` and `ComposeStencils`. 2) Additional methods were added for determining the `BroadcastStyle`s of `DataLayout` broadcasts that have identical layouts but different base types, along with a method for determining the `BroadcastStyle`s of `Field` broadcasts that have different `DataLayout`s. This was to allow broadcasts over similar `Field`s with different base types; e.g., a `Field` of `Float64`s and a `Field` of `Dual`s. This made it easier to implement the Jacobian test.

It should be noted that the "storing-stencils-as-Fields" strategy is only a temporary solution. Specifically, broadcasts that act on stencil fields can be rather counterintuitive. Although `stencil_field1.^2 .+ stencil_field2 .* scalar_field` currently works as expected, that is only because I have overloaded basic arithmetic operations over `StencilCoefs` objects to automatically map over the objects. Trying to do more general operations like `f.(stencil_field)` will not work as expected, since `f` will be passed `StencilCoefs` objects, rather than the individual coefficients. In order to fix this problem, we need an alternative to a `Field` that is specifically designed for storing matrix-like objects, rather than vector-like objects. Such a data structure would also probably be useful for the coupler. Alternatively, we could define a new `BroadcastStyle` for stencil fields that automatically maps any function over `StencilCoefs` objects, though this would not be a very generalizable solution.

In the near future I will also add the stencil operation `stencil_solve!(x, stencil, b)`, which determines the `Field` `x` for which `ApplyStencil().(stencil, x) = b`. This will get used in the linear solver, which is currently the last place where I access parent arrays of `Field`s for non-debugging purposes, and will allow the solver to handle non-tridiagonal Schur complement matrices. Note that `stencil_solve!` will not be an `Operator`, since it must scan the entirety of `stencil` and `b` to determine the value of `x` at a particular point; it will be more akin to functions like `weighted_dss!`.
title: Implicit Solver Experimentation
